# **learneasy Learn: Comprehensive Product Specification and Technical Architecture**

## **1\. Executive Overview and Strategic Vision**

The global educational technology ecosystem currently serves a vast demographic of learners, yet it persistently marginalizes individuals with neurodevelopmental conditions. Globally, over 240 million children have special educational needs, and in India alone, this encompasses approximately 35 million children navigating conditions such as Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), and specific learning disabilities.1 The systemic support infrastructure is critically under-resourced, burdened by a staggering ratio of one special educator for every 250 children.1 Consequently, educators are overwhelmed by manual workflows, parents face profound stigmatization and confusion, and neurodivergent children are frequently alienated from formal educational pathways.1 learneasy Learn is engineered specifically to dismantle these barriers by providing an autism-first, technologically advanced educational platform aligned with the National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) Open Basic Education (OBE) curriculum.3  
Traditional digital learning environments operate on pedagogical assumptions that inadvertently create prohibitive barriers for learners on the autism spectrum. These platforms presume strong verbal comprehension, sustained executive functioning, high tolerance for sensory stimuli, and the capacity for independent, self-directed learning.3 learneasy Learn subverts these paradigms by synthesizing specialized cognitive accessibility guidelines, adaptive probabilistic modeling, and secure artificial intelligence architecture. The platform combines structured curriculum delivery, dynamic artificial intelligence tutoring, verifiable parental involvement, and data-driven educator collaboration into a singular, unified learning ecosystem.3  
This document serves as an exhaustive product specification and technical architectural blueprint, designed for immediate handover to software engineering, UX design, and product management teams. It comprehensively delineates the platform's information architecture, user journey topologies, spatial wireframe logic, neurodivergent-specific user experience guidelines, and the underlying mathematical frameworks governing the mastery models. Furthermore, it details the strict compliance mechanisms required under India’s Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the multi-layered safety guardrails required for generative AI interactions with minors, and the financial integrations necessary for sustainable monetization in the Indian market.4 The overarching technical objective is to deploy a scalable, highly predictable, and hyper-individualized digital infrastructure that empowers autistic learners to achieve verifiable academic mastery without inducing cognitive overload or sensory fatigue.

## **2\. Autism-Specific User Experience (UX) Guidelines**

Designing digital interfaces for neurodivergent populations requires a fundamental departure from conventional, gamification-heavy interface models. Standard accessibility compliance, such as the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1, is largely optimized for motor, visual, and auditory impairments.8 While foundational, these guidelines often leave cognitive accessibility as a secondary consideration, meaning that a strictly WCAG-compliant interface can still be overwhelmingly confusing or anxiety-inducing for a user with ASD or ADHD.8 To truly design inclusively, product engineering must shift its primary metric from mere "task completion" to the facilitation of emotional comfort, mental energy conservation, and autonomous navigation.8  
The foremost principle governing the learneasy Learn interface is the aggressive reduction of visual and cognitive overload. When too many competing elements occupy a single screen, they fight for the user’s limited attentional resources, resulting in cognitive overwhelm and subsequent task abandonment.8 The design system must enforce simplified page layouts, strictly limit unnecessary visual stimulation, and prioritize an unambiguous visual hierarchy.8 Predictability and consistency are paramount. Unexpected changes in layout, sudden modal pop-ups, or unpredictable navigational shifts act as immediate triggers for disorientation and anxiety in autistic users.8 Therefore, navigation menus, search functionalities, and primary interactive elements must remain anchored in exact, unchanging coordinates across every single page within the application.9  
Sensory management dictates the application's color theory and kinetic behavior. Autistic users frequently experience hyper-reactivity to sensory input. Consequently, the interface must deploy soft, muted color palettes, explicitly avoiding high-luminance neon colors, stark pure white, or absolute black backgrounds.9 Motion and sound require absolute user control. Auto-playing media, moving background elements, and non-initiated audio severely disrupt focus and cause acute sensory overload.8 The system must prohibit flashing animations and provide explicit, easily accessible options for users to pause, stop, or mute any kinetic or auditory feedback.8 Furthermore, the application must eliminate any time-sensitive interactions. Time countdowns, expiring sessions, and artificial urgency mechanisms dramatically escalate cognitive load and anxiety, and are therefore strictly forbidden within the learning modules.8  
Linguistic clarity forms the final pillar of the UX framework. Autistic individuals frequently interpret language with strict literalness. The interface microcopy must categorically exclude idioms, metaphors, sarcasm, and complex conceptual jargon.9 Instructions must be formulated with high directness, utilizing plain-language standards.11 To accommodate varied cognitive processing styles, the interface must never rely solely on iconography; every interactive icon must be accompanied by explicit text labels.10

## **3\. Information Architecture**

The Information Architecture (IA) of learneasy Learn is deliberately flat and deterministic, designed to minimize the cognitive friction required to locate specific functionalities. Complex, multi-stage hierarchical menus are avoided in favor of direct, predictable pathways.12 The architecture is divided into three distinct, role-based environments: the Student Learning Interface, the Parent Consent and Tracking Dashboard, and the Educator Intervention Portal.  
The Student Learning Interface is the core operational environment. It completely isolates the learner from administrative functions, account settings, or complex data visualizations. Upon authentication, the student is presented with a singular, highly structured path representing their current Individualized Education Program (IEP). This path eliminates the paradox of choice. Instead of a vast library of courses, the student sees a linear progression node—for example, a module on "Counting 1-10" within the NIOS Level A Mathematics curriculum.3 The architecture ensures that learning tasks are broken into micro-flows, adhering to the principle of presenting only one concept at a time.3  
The Parent Dashboard is architected as a dual-purpose environment managing both legal compliance and educational monitoring. The primary architectural node within this dashboard is the Consent Management System (CMS), which handles the cryptographic verification of parental identity and the granular administration of data processing permissions.4 Parallel to the CMS is the Analytics Node, which translates the student's raw interaction telemetry into accessible, plain-language weekly reports and visual progress charts.3  
The Educator Intervention Portal features a more complex architecture designed for data density, catering to special educators and occupational therapists. This portal connects the student's mastery states with the broader NIOS Open Basic Education framework. It houses the Goal Assignment engine, the RAG-powered AI Intervention Recommendation engine, and the summative reporting tools necessary for institutional compliance and localized educational planning.1 The separation of these three environments ensures that no single user is exposed to information or controls outside their specific operational requirements, thereby preserving systemic simplicity and cognitive ease.

## **4\. Wireframe Spatial Logic and Interaction Design**

Given the textual nature of this specification, the wireframe logic is articulated through strict spatial rules and coordinate constraints rather than visual mockups. The interface geometry is designed to respect the cognitive accessibility requirement of extreme predictability.8  
The spatial topology of the primary student learning interface is divided into three immutable horizontal strata. The uppermost stratum is the Global Control Header. This area is restricted to persistent, non-intrusive elements, most notably a clearly labeled "Exit to Home" button located in the top-left coordinate. This fulfills the critical psychological requirement of providing users with an immediate, easily discoverable escape route from overwhelming tasks, thereby maintaining user autonomy and emotional comfort.8 The top-right coordinate is reserved for a simple, non-distracting progress indicator, utilizing a segmented bar rather than percentage text, helping users understand task boundaries without inducing performance anxiety.8  
The middle stratum, comprising the vast majority of the screen real estate, is the Central Canvas. This area is exclusively dedicated to the active learning object. Whether the activity involves visual counting, matching, or sequencing, the content must be centered and isolated by expansive white space (or muted negative space). This strict isolation prevents the user's attention from being dragged to peripheral elements, dramatically reducing cognitive overwhelm.8  
The lowest stratum is the Action Footer. This section houses the primary progression mechanisms, such as the "Check Answer" or "Next" buttons. By physically separating the input mechanisms from the learning content, the interface establishes a clean, logical visual hierarchy.8 Buttons within this footer must possess large hit areas to accommodate potential fine-motor difficulties, and their active states must rely on high-contrast changes rather than subtle drop-shadows or color shifts.12  
Error recovery mechanisms within these wireframes demand specialized interaction design. Encountering errors can trigger intense frustration and emotional dysregulation for neurodivergent learners, frequently leading to immediate task abandonment.8 Consequently, traditional failure indicators—such as harsh red typography, large "X" icons, or negative auditory buzzers—are strictly prohibited. When an incorrect input is detected, the interface must deploy graceful, non-blaming recovery protocols.8 The system should provide gentle, constructive visual hints or simplified re-explanations, framing the event as a continuation of the learning process rather than a definitive failure.3

## **5\. User Journeys and Dynamic Workflows**

User journeys map the sequential interactions of the platform's primary personas, ensuring that systemic behavior aligns with both psychological needs and strict legal frameworks.

### **5.1 The Parent Onboarding and Compliance Journey**

The parent onboarding sequence is perhaps the most critical administrative journey, as it must seamlessly integrate user education with the stringent legal requirements of India's Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023\.13 Under the DPDP Act, platforms processing the personal data of children (individuals under 18\) are legally mandated to obtain verifiable parental consent (VPC) prior to any data collection.4 This journey cannot rely on traditional, frictionless "click-through" terms of service.  
The journey commences when the parent initiates account creation. The system explicitly blocks the creation of the child's profile until the adult's identity is cryptographically verified. The application routes the parent to an integrated verification gateway utilizing India's digital public infrastructure, such as Aadhaar-based e-KYC or the DigiLocker API.4 This step legally proves that the operator is an adult and establishes the guardian relationship.4 Following successful verification, the parent enters the Consent Management System (CMS) module. Here, the DPDP Act prohibits bundled consent; the parent cannot be forced to accept all data processing activities through a single toggle.4  
The interface presents the parent with granular, itemized consent requests.4 The parent is asked to provide an explicit, affirmative action (e.g., actively toggling a switch from off to on, as pre-ticked boxes are illegal) for specific purposes, such as core educational telemetry, AI model utilization, and aggregated institutional reporting.4 Upon submission, the backend generates an immutable Consent Artifact—a digitally signed, timestamped record that serves as legal proof of authorization.4 Only after this artifact is logged in the database is the parent permitted to proceed to create the child's specific learning profile, detailing their NIOS level, sensory sensitivities, and reading comprehension abilities.3

### **5.2 The Student Daily Learning Journey**

The student's daily journey is engineered for cognitive ease. Upon logging in, the system reads the student's profile and automatically applies their specific environmental settings, such as activating a "low-stimulation" mode that mutes colors and simplifies typography.8 The student is not presented with a dashboard, but rather a direct, singular "Start Learning" pathway.  
Clicking this initiates a micro-flow based on the next unmastered node in their curriculum. The journey follows a predictable, recurring five-step pedagogical sequence.3 Step one is Observation, where the concept is introduced visually. Step two involves Guided Practice, allowing interactive learning with proactive system hints. Step three is Independent Practice, requiring the student to solve problems without algorithmic assistance. Step four is the Mastery Check, deploying three to five assessment questions to gauge retention. The final step is Positive Completion, providing simple, literal encouragement (e.g., "Great work. You counted correctly.") without triggering overwhelming celebratory animations.3 Once the flow concludes, the student is gently routed back to the exit node, ensuring a clear start and end point that prevents the anxiety of endless processes.8

### **5.3 The Educator Monitoring Journey**

The educator's journey focuses on translating macro-level data into micro-level interventions. The educator logs into the institutional portal and accesses a roster of assigned students. By selecting a specific student, the educator reviews the AI-generated insights regarding the student's recent sessions. If the system flags a high error rate in a specific mathematical concept, the educator can initiate an intervention workflow. They can manually adjust the student's scaffolding settings, increasing visual hints for the upcoming week, or assign a specific remedial objective linked directly to the NIOS curriculum framework.3 The educator then generates an automated, DPDP-compliant progress report to share with the parents, ensuring collaborative, data-driven support.3

## **6\. Curriculum Content Model and Educational Equivalency**

The fundamental academic architecture of learneasy Learn is rigorously aligned with the National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) Open Basic Education (OBE) curriculum. This alignment ensures that the platform is not merely a supplementary therapeutic tool, but a formalized educational pathway that provides recognized equivalency to the traditional Indian schooling system.3 The OBE programme is specifically designed to be flexible and accessible, making it the optimal framework for learners with developmental disabilities who require alternative pacing and assessment mechanisms.19  
The curriculum is structured across three primary hierarchical tiers, mapped to developmental competencies rather than strict chronological ages.3 The content model mandates that every learning objective within these tiers is atomized into discrete, manageable nodes to support the platform's adaptive algorithms.

### **6.1 Level A Curriculum Architecture**

Level A provides foundational education equivalent to Classes 1 through 3 of the formal school system.3 This level serves as the entry point for children with significant learning delays or early-stage academic autistic learners who have not yet established reliable numeracy or literacy.20 The content model for Level A encompasses Mathematics, Environmental Studies (EVS), Basic Computer Skills, and Language.21  
Within the database schema, a Level A Mathematics node might focus on basic number recognition, counting from 1 to 10, identifying fundamental geometric shapes, and single-digit addition and subtraction.3 Pedagogically, the content model dictates that instruction at this level relies heavily on visual-first methodologies. Abstract numerical concepts are invariably introduced through concrete, visual representations—such as matching an integer to a corresponding quantity of physical objects—before introducing symbolic arithmetic.3

### **6.2 Level B Curriculum Architecture**

Level B corresponds to intermediate elementary education, equivalent to Classes 4 and 5\.3 This tier builds sequentially upon the foundations of Level A, continuing with Mathematics, EVS, Basic Computer Skills, and Language.21  
The curriculum schema for Level B introduces heightened cognitive demands. The Mathematics syllabus transitions into multi-digit addition and subtraction, fundamental multiplication and division, the introduction of fractions and decimals, basic mensuration, and elementary geometry.23 At this stage, the content model begins to integrate more complex sequencing and logic-based activities, challenging the learner to manipulate data while still providing robust scaffolding to manage executive functioning limitations.3

### **6.3 Level C Curriculum Architecture**

Level C represents advanced elementary education, equivalent to Classes 6 through 8\.3 This level significantly diversifies the subject matter, preplearneasyg the student for secondary educational pathways. The required subjects expand to include English Language, Mathematics, Science, Social Science, and Basic Computer Skills.21  
The content model for Level C demands rigorous semantic structuring within the database. The Science curriculum introduces concepts such as magnetism, electric currents, force, friction, sound, light, natural phenomena, and waste management.25 Concurrently, the Social Science curriculum requires students to comprehend agriculture, industrial development, modes of transportation, population dynamics, the Indian Constitution, and local self-governance.26 Because these subjects involve highly abstract concepts, the platform's AI generation workflows are heavily utilized at this level to parse complex texts into simplified, literal narratives that bypass the metaphorical language often found in standard textbooks.3

## **7\. Mastery Model: Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT)**

The pedagogical engine driving learneasy Learn's adaptive capabilities is founded on Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT). BKT is a probabilistic model grounded in a hidden Markov framework, initially formalized by Corbett and Anderson, and it serves as the principal algorithmic mechanism for mastery learning in intelligent tutoring systems.27 Unlike classical summative assessments or simple percentage-based scoring, BKT operates on the premise that student knowledge is a latent, unobservable state—a binary variable representing whether a specific skill is mastered or unmastered.27 The algorithm infers this hidden state by sequentially observing the correctness of the student's interactions over time.30

### **7.1 The Four Parameters of BKT**

The mathematical architecture of BKT is governed by four distinct, interpretable probabilistic parameters that model the dynamics of skill acquisition.27

| Parameter | Notation | Description and Pedagogical Application |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Initial Mastery** | **![][image1]** | The probability that the learner already possesses knowledge of the skill prior to their first interaction with the system.31 |
| **Probability of Learning** | **![][image2]** | The probability that a learner will transition from an unmastered state to a mastered state after engaging in a practice opportunity.31 |
| **Probability of Guessing** | **![][image3]** | The probability that a learner produces a correct response despite lacking true mastery of the underlying skill.31 |
| **Probability of Slipping** | **![][image4]** | The probability that a learner produces an incorrect response despite having fully mastered the skill.31 |

### **7.2 Algorithmic State Updates**

As the student interacts with a curriculum node, the algorithm continuously recalculates the probability of mastery (![][image5]) based on the observable evidence (![][image6]). If the student applies the skill correctly (![][image7]), the conditional probability is updated utilizing Bayes' theorem to account for the possibility that the correct answer was a mere guess 30:  
![][image8]  
34  
Conversely, if the student answers incorrectly (![][image9]), the model updates the probability to account for the likelihood that the failure was a mechanical slip rather than a lack of knowledge 30:  
![][image10]  
34  
Following the incorporation of the observational evidence, the system calculates the final prior probability for the subsequent interaction by factoring in the overarching probability of learning transition:  
![][image11]  
29

### **7.3 Relevance to Neurodivergent Education**

The application of BKT is particularly revolutionary in the context of special education and autism. Standard educational models penalize every incorrect answer equally. However, autistic learners frequently experience fine-motor deficits, severe attention fluctuations, or acute sensory distractions that cause them to input incorrect answers even when they fully comprehend the mathematical or scientific concept.8  
In the learneasy Learn architecture, the "Slip" parameter (![][image4]) is dynamically calibrated to accommodate this reality. If a student's historical telemetry indicates a high frequency of erratic inputs despite previous demonstrations of mastery, the model attributes subsequent errors to slips rather than conceptual regression.33 This prevents the system from unfairly demoting the student to easier, remedial tasks, which can cause frustration and boredom. Instead, the adaptive engine maintains the conceptual difficulty while simultaneously altering the interface scaffolding—perhaps reducing visual clutter or removing a drag-and-drop mechanism in favor of a simpler multiple-choice selection—to reduce the likelihood of further mechanical slips.3

## **8\. Artificial Intelligence Architecture and RAG Implementation**

The integration of Generative Artificial Intelligence within learneasy Learn is engineered to function as a "Cognitive Mirror" and instructional scaffold, deliberately moving away from the paradigm of the AI as an omniscient oracle that simply outputs correct answers.36 The architectural objective is to provide immediate, highly personalized educational support—such as simplified explanations, contextual examples, and guided hints—that adapts to the learner's immediate cognitive load and sensory preferences.3  
To achieve this without suffering from the hallucination risks inherent in raw Large Language Models (LLMs), the platform utilizes a sophisticated Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline.3 This hybrid architecture connects a highly deterministic vector database with the generative capabilities of models like GPT-4o or Anthropic's Claude.38  
The foundation of the RAG pipeline is a PostgreSQL database integrated with the pgvector extension.3 The entirety of the NIOS OBE curriculum—comprising every syllabus objective, pedagogical rule, and approved reading passage—is chunked and processed through an embedding model to create high-dimensional vector representations.39 These vectors are stored securely within pgvector.40  
When a student requires an AI intervention, the generation workflow is triggered. The system does not merely send the user's question to the LLM. First, the intent and context are captured. The backend executes a semantic similarity search within the pgvector database to retrieve the exact, approved curriculum nodes relevant to the current task.37 Concurrently, the system retrieves the student's current BKT mastery state and their specific cognitive profile from the relational database schema.38  
This localized data is then assembled into a strict system prompt. The prompt structurally binds the LLM, instructing it to synthesize a response based solely on the retrieved curriculum facts, while adjusting the linguistic complexity, sentence length, and formatting to match the student's profile.38 For example, if the student profile indicates high sensory sensitivity and literal interpretation, the prompt forces the LLM to strip all metaphors from its explanation and output instructions in distinct, highly explicit micro-steps.3 The resulting output is mathematically grounded in the approved curriculum, ensuring absolute pedagogical accuracy and alignment with NIOS standards.

## **9\. AI Safety and Guardrails for Minors**

The deployment of conversational Generative AI within an educational technology platform interacting directly with vulnerable minors necessitates uncompromising, multi-layered security architectures. Traditional content moderation systems, which rely on simple keyword blocking, are entirely insufficient to handle the novel risks introduced by prompt manipulation, synthetic content generation, and sophisticated jailbreak attempts.42 learneasy Learn mitigates these critical vulnerabilities through the deployment of system-level AI safety shields, primarily utilizing Meta’s PromptGuard and Llama Guard 3 frameworks.6  
The safety architecture operates as an asynchronous, bidirectional gateway intercepting all data flowing to and from the core generative model.6

### **9.1 Layer 1: Input Sanitization via PromptGuard**

The first line of defense is PromptGuard, a lightweight microservice explicitly designed to neutralize malicious prompt engineering.6 Before a user's text input reaches the primary LLM or the RAG retrieval mechanism, it is parsed by PromptGuard. This model classifies the input to detect embedded prompt injections (where untrusted data attempts to hijack the model's instructions) or direct jailbreaks (where malicious instructions attempt to explicitly override the AI's built-in safety constraints, e.g., "Ignore all previous instructions and enter developer mode").6 If an injection vector is detected, the input is immediately blocked, and the transaction is securely logged for administrative review.

### **9.2 Layer 2: Bidirectional Safety Filtering via Llama Guard 3**

Inputs that pass the initial sanitization, as well as the subsequent responses generated by the core LLM, are aggressively evaluated by Llama Guard 3\.6 Operating as an independent 8-billion parameter safety classifier fine-tuned on the MLCommons AI Safety taxonomy, Llama Guard 3 assesses content across specific, highly defined hazard categories.43  
Within the learneasy Learn operational context, several of these hazard categories are of critical importance:

* **S4: Child Sexual Exploitation & S3: Sex-Related Crimes:** Generative AI chatbots have documented vulnerabilities where they can be manipulated into generating grooming-like dialogue or sexually explicit text involving minors.42 Llama Guard 3 strictly analyzes both user prompts and model outputs for these patterns, neutralizing any interaction that borders on exploitation.46  
* **S11: Suicide & Self-Harm:** Autistic youth experience significantly higher rates of anxiety, depression, and emotional dysregulation. The safety shield continuously scans conversational inputs for expressions of self-harm or despair.47 Crucially, it prohibits the AI from generating any instructions or encouragement related to self-injury, instead triggering a secure backend protocol that alerts designated educators or guardians to the emotional distress.42  
* **S10: Hate Speech & S5: Defamation:** To protect the psychological well-being of the learners, the output guardrails ensure the AI never generates discriminatory, derogatory, or biased language that could induce trauma or damage self-esteem.46  
* **S6: Specialized Advice:** The AI Tutor is an educational tool, not a medical professional. The guardrail strictly prevents the model from dispensing unauthorized medical, psychological, or psychiatric advice regarding autism therapies, medication dosages, or legal guidance, which poses a massive liability risk.6

If Llama Guard 3 flags a generated response as violative of any categorized policy, the platform’s orchestration layer immediately intercepts the payload. It suppresses the harmful text and dynamically swaps it with a pre-approved, generic safety response (e.g., "I cannot fulfill this request. Let's return to the lesson on fractions.").46

## **10\. Database Schema Architecture**

The data architecture of learneasy Learn relies on a centralized PostgreSQL infrastructure. To satisfy the complex requirements of both adaptive learning algorithms and strict regulatory compliance under the DPDP Act, the database employs a highly normalized, hybrid schema. It seamlessly manages traditional relational data, immutable audit logs for consent, and high-dimensional vectors for AI retrieval.41  
The paramount architectural directive is the stringent separation of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) from analytical telemetry and AI processing.49 This ensures that if analytical data sets are utilized for research or model training, the identities of the minors remain cryptographically insulated.

### **10.1 Relational Data and State Management**

**Table: GuardianAccounts** This table stores the cryptographically verified details of the adult guardian, essential for DPDP compliance.4

| Column Name | Data Type | Constraints | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| guardian\_id | UUID | Primary Key | Unique identifier for the parent. |
| kyc\_hash | VARCHAR | Not Null | Hashed verification proof from DigiLocker/Aadhaar.4 |
| contact\_email | VARCHAR | Unique | Encrypted contact point for system notices. |

**Table: StudentProfiles** This table contains the pseudonymized educational settings for the learner. It does not contain direct identifiers like names or birthdates.49

| Column Name | Data Type | Constraints | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| profile\_id | UUID | Primary Key | Pseudonymized routing identifier. |
| guardian\_id | UUID | Foreign Key | Maps the student to the verified guardian. |
| nios\_level | VARCHAR(2) | Not Null | Curriculum mapping (e.g., "A", "B", "C").3 |
| sensory\_sensitivity | BOOLEAN | Default FALSE | Toggles low-stimulation UX rendering.3 |
| reading\_level | VARCHAR(10) | Not Null | Determines the ratio of text to visual UI elements.3 |

**Table: BKT\_Mastery\_States** This table stores the dynamic probabilistic outputs of the Bayesian Knowledge Tracing algorithm, updated after every interaction.27

| Column Name | Data Type | Constraints | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| state\_id | UUID | Primary Key | Unique log entry for the state calculation. |
| profile\_id | UUID | Foreign Key | Linked student profile. |
| concept\_id | UUID | Foreign Key | Linked specific NIOS learning objective. |
| p\_mastery | FLOAT | Range 0.0-1.0 | Current probability of mastery (![][image5]).50 |
| p\_slip\_dynamic | FLOAT | Range 0.0-1.0 | The dynamically calculated slip parameter (![][image4]).33 |
| timestamp | TIMESTAMP | Not Null | Exact time of the state calculation. |

### **10.2 Compliance and Vector Storage**

**Table: DPDP\_Consent\_Artifacts** This table serves as the immutable ledger for all legal permissions granted or revoked by the guardian, acting as the system's core defense during regulatory audits.4

| Column Name | Data Type | Constraints | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| artifact\_id | UUID | Primary Key | Unique digital signature for the consent action.4 |
| guardian\_id | UUID | Foreign Key | The parent executing the consent. |
| purpose\_id | VARCHAR | Not Null | Granular definition of data use (e.g., "Core\_Telemetry", "AI\_Processing").4 |
| status | VARCHAR | Not Null | Current state: "Active", "Expired", "Withdrawn".4 |
| audit\_timestamp | TIMESTAMP | Not Null | Irrefutable timestamp of the action.4 |

**Table: Curriculum\_Vector\_Store (pgvector)** This table stores the unstructured semantic data required by the RAG AI architecture.37

| Column Name | Data Type | Constraints | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| node\_id | UUID | Primary Key | Identifier for the curriculum chunk. |
| nios\_mapping | VARCHAR | Not Null | Metadata linking to the formal syllabus. |
| content\_text | TEXT | Not Null | Raw text of the educational material. |
| embedding | VECTOR(1536) | Indexed | High-dimensional numerical vector enabling semantic similarity searches by the LLM.37 |

## **11\. Learning Analytics and Educator Workflows**

While the student interface minimizes data visualization to prevent overload, the backend aggregates profound analytical depth for the Educator Workflows. Special educators and occupational therapists require transparent, empirical data to optimize Individualized Education Programs (IEPs) and communicate progress effectively.1  
The learning analytics engine captures high-resolution telemetry from every student interaction without violating the DPDP Act's data minimization constraints, as the data collected is strictly necessary for the specified educational purpose.3 The system logs precise variables including response accuracy, latency (time-on-task), hint invocation frequency, and retry sequences.3  
This raw telemetry fuels the Educator Dashboard. The dashboard translates complex BKT state arrays into actionable pedagogical insights. If the algorithm detects that a student's learning rate parameter (![][image2]) has stalled over successive sessions regarding a specific mathematical concept, the system proactively generates an intervention alert.3 It suggests actionable adjustments, such as shifting the student from a text-heavy instructional mode to a highly visual, manipulative-based modality. Furthermore, educators utilize the dashboard to execute Goal Assignments, directly linking specific NIOS syllabus chapters to a student's queue, and subsequently generating exportable, visually coherent progress reports suitable for institutional filing and parent-teacher consultations.3

## **12\. Monetization Strategy and API Specifications**

Delivering high-fidelity special education technology requires a sustainable commercial model. The overarching monetization strategy must navigate the economic realities of the Indian market, where the prohibitive costs of physical special education—often ranging from INR 250,000 to 600,000 annually—render traditional services financially inaccessible for the vast majority of the population.2  
learneasy Learn deploys a freemium and tiered subscription model. Competitive market analysis of existing Indian special education applications indicates that optimal, mass-market subscription pricing falls between INR 4,999 and INR 11,999 annually.51 A Basic Tier (Free) offers introductory access to early Level A mathematics and functional communication tools, establishing immediate user value. The Premium Tier, projected at approximately INR 5,999 per year or INR 699 per month, unlocks the complete NIOS OBE Level A, B, and C curriculum, activates the personalized AI Tutor, and provides access to advanced parental analytics.51 An Institutional Tier offers volume-based API licensing for schools and specialized therapy centers.1

### **12.1 Financial Integration via Razorpay API**

To execute seamless recurring billing within the complex Indian regulatory environment, the platform integrates deeply with Razorpay's Subscriptions and UPI Autopay APIs.54 The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) mandates strict electronic mandate (e-mandate) rules for all recurring payments, requiring explicit user authorization to prevent renewal failures.56 Razorpay abstracts this complexity by natively managing mandate creation, dispatching mandatory pre-debit notifications, and executing intelligent retry logic for failed transactions.54  
When a guardian initiates a Premium subscription via the frontend application, the backend Node.js service constructs and transmits a POST request to the Razorpay Subscriptions API to instantiate the payment plan.7  
**API Request Specification: Create Subscription**

JSON  
POST https://api.razorpay.com/v1/subscriptions  
Content-Type: application/json  
Authorization: Basic \[Encoded\_Credentials\]

{  
  "plan\_id": "plan\_Premium\_Annual\_OBE\_01",  
  "total\_count": 1,  
  "quantity": 1,  
  "start\_at": 1735689600,  
  "expire\_by": 1735776000,  
  "customer\_notify": 1,  
  "addons":,  
  "notes": {  
    "guardian\_id": "uuid-8472-abcd",  
    "subscription\_tier": "Premium\_Annual",  
    "compliance\_region": "IN"  
  }  
}

Upon successful transmission, the Razorpay API returns a JSON response containing the unique id of the subscription and its status.7 The learneasy Learn backend captures this subscription\_id and binds it to the GuardianAccounts record in the PostgreSQL database.  
**API Response Specification**

JSON  
{  
  "id": "sub\_00000000000001",  
  "entity": "subscription",  
  "plan\_id": "plan\_Premium\_Annual\_OBE\_01",  
  "status": "created",  
  "current\_start": null,  
  "current\_end": null,  
  "ended\_at": null,  
  "quantity": 1,  
  "notes": {  
    "guardian\_id": "uuid-8472-abcd",  
    "subscription\_tier": "Premium\_Annual",  
    "compliance\_region": "IN"  
  },  
  "short\_url": "https://rzp.io/i/Xyz123"  
}

To ensure operational continuity without manual reconciliation, the system relies heavily on Razorpay Webhooks.57 The backend infrastructure listens for specific event triggers, such as subscription.charged indicating a successful renewal, or subscription.halted indicating a mandate failure.57 Upon receiving a webhook payload, the system automatically queries the internal database, cross-references the notes.guardian\_id, and dynamically updates the user's access rights and premium status across the application. This ensures that the financial architecture operates with the same level of automation and reliability as the educational and compliance frameworks.

## **13\. Conclusion**

The architectural and pedagogical frameworks outlined in this specification establish learneasy Learn as a profoundly disruptive force in the special education technology sector. By aggressively translating clinical neurodivergent UX principles directly into spatial wireframe logic, the platform successfully dismantles the cognitive fatigue and sensory overload inherent in traditional digital learning environments. The integration of Bayesian Knowledge Tracing provides a mathematically rigorous, probabilistic foundation for hyper-personalized pacing. By dynamically modeling the unique behavioral variances—specifically the mechanical slips and correct guesses—frequently exhibited by autistic learners, the system ensures that academic progression is based on genuine conceptual mastery rather than rote interaction.  
Simultaneously, the platform's backend infrastructure is fortified to meet stringent demands for scalability, legal compliance, and artificial intelligence safety. The Retrieval-Augmented Generation architecture, securely enveloped by the multi-layered defenses of PromptGuard and Llama Guard 3, guarantees that generative educational interventions remain clinically safe, pedagogically verified, and entirely free from algorithmic harm or exploitation. The uncompromising adherence to the DPDP Act 2023, facilitated through immutable Consent Artifacts and cryptographic guardian verification, establishes a definitive standard for data ethics in the EdTech ecosystem. Finally, the strategic integration of localized financial infrastructure via Razorpay's UPI Autopay APIs ensures frictionless, compliant monetization, positioning learneasy Learn for rapid, sustainable market penetration across India. This document provides the exhaustive technical, logical, and structural framework necessary for immediate and precise engineering deployment.
